Experience the best of Hanoi in just 4 days with this ultimate itinerary! After living in Hanoi for over 8 months, I’ve put together a guide featuring the city’s highlights and nearby must-visit destinations like Ha Long Bay and Ninh Binh.  

**What to Expect:**  
- Wander the vibrant streets of Hanoi’s Old Quarter and relax by Hoan Kiem Lake  
- Try iconic egg coffee, bánh mì, and phở  
- Visit Train Street, the Temple of Literature, and bustling night markets  
- Take unforgettable day trips to Ha Long Bay and Ninh Binh (Tam Coc, Hang Mua)

Hanoi is the capital city of Vietnam, located in the northern part of the country. It’s a chaotic but fascinating city, with motorcycles weaving through bustling traffic and the constant sound of honking horns. It’s a blend of both old and modern Vietnam, where ancient pagoda’s and well-preserved colonial buildings stand along tall skyscrapers. When you add to that an excellent food scene, vibrant nightlife, and rich cultural heritage, you’ve got a captivating city. In this video, we highlight 12 of our favorite things to do in Hanoi.

videos.transcript: What's up guys we're here in Hanoi and in this video we're going to show you our top things to do here as well as some essential travel tips that will help you navigate this busy city. So hope you enjoy it. Hanoi is the capital city of Vietnam located in the northern part of the country. It's a chaotic but fascinating city with motorcycles weaving through bustling traffic and the constant sound of honking horns. It's a blend of both old and modern Vietnam where ancient pagodas and well-preserved colonial buildings stand along tall skyscrapers. When you add to that an excellent food scene vibrant nightlife and rich cultural heritage, you've got a captivating city. In this video, we highlight 12 of our favorite things to do in Hanoi. Make sure to watch until the end as we also share a few additional things that we learned that will help you plan your trip. Now let's dive in. We are Zac and Ine. We make helpful videos about destinations around the world so you can easily plan your own adventures. The Old Quarter is the tourist hub of Hanoi known for its narrow winding streets and colonial architecture. Here, you can find a wealth of hotels, restaurants, bars, travel agencies, and shops. As you navigate the streets of the Old Quarter, the sights, sounds, and aromas will overwhelm your senses. There are also a few key landmarks to visit here, such as St. Joseph's Cathedral and the Heritage House. The latter gives you an opportunity to step back in time as you visit a traditional merchant's house with beautifully decorated rooms. We visited this residence as part of a free walking tour and we left a link to the one that we booked in the description below the video. When the night falls, it's worth checking out Beer Street, a vibrant hub of drinks and entertainment in the heart of the Old Quarter. Both tourists and locals flock to this bustling area to immerse themselves in the electric nightlife scene and enjoy the cuisine. This busy street is filled with a wide range of bars, pubs, and restaurants. Go on a Friday or Saturday for the best atmosphere with live music and the streets closed to traffic. Located at the edge of the Old Quarter, Hoan Kiem Lake holds significant cultural and historical importance. Surrounding the lake is a tranquil park where you can enjoy a leisurely stroll or simply soak in the peaceful atmosphere. It's a great escape from the bustling streets of Hanoi and takes about 25 minutes if you walk the whole Loop without stopping. One of the main attractions at the lake is the Ngoc Son Temple, perched on a small island and connected by a small red bridge. Dating back to the 18th century, the temple is dedicated to the Vietnamese leader who defeated the Mongols in the 13th century. Train Street is one of the most popular sites in Hanoi. This small area sees trains pass multiple times per day with homes and coffee shops lining the tracks. It used to be possible to sip coffee in one of the cafes while waiting for the train to pass by, but unfortunately due to safety concerns, walking on the tracks is now forbidden. It is still possible to take a picture from one of the intersecting streets, but the entrance is blocked. Adjacent to train Street you can find Phung Hung Mural Street, stretching for about 220 meters and showcasing the rich cultural heritage of Hanoi through street art. As you walk along the street, you can find yourself embarking on a journey back in time. You'll be immersed in a beautiful visual storytelling experience that celebrates the city's growth throughout its history. No trip to Hanoi would be complete without seeing the tomb of Vietnam's beloved revolutionary leader Hồ Chí Minh. Foreign tourists and Vietnamese locals queue for hours every morning to pay their respects to his body, which is encased in a glass sarcophagus. You can easily combine your visit with the remainder of monuments in the park such as Hồ Chí Minh Museum, One Pillar Pagoda, and Stilt House. The latter is a small wooden stilt house of two floors where the leader used to live and work. It's a great opportunity to learn about Vietnam's political history. Make sure to wear something that covers your knees when you enter the mausoleum because it's not allowed to come in shorts. I had the same problem and I had to buy like a skirt around the corner for 50,000 Dong. For a truly authentic shopping experience, you should head to one of the markets in the city. Dong Xuan is a large indoor market that offers a wide range of goods and products. Here, you can find everything from local foods and fresh produce to clothing accessories and souvenirs. If you're visiting the city during the weekend, you can also visit the Weekend Night Market in the Old Quarter. This market starts from Hang Dao Street and stretches to the edge of Dong Xuan Market. It's open on Friday, Saturday, and Sunday from 7:00 p.m. until late. The Weekend Market caters more towards tourists with traditional Vietnamese crafts, souvenirs, street food, and cultural performances. West Lake is Hanoi's largest lake, spanning about 15 kilometers. It's a quiet break from the city where you can go cycling or take a stroll. There are two pagodas to visit at West Lake and Tran Quoc Pagoda is the most popular one. This Buddhist temple is one of the oldest of its kind and features a stunning seven-story pagoda. It's free to enter, but remember to dress conservatively here. The West Lake area also has many restaurants and cafes where you can relax and enjoy the serene surroundings. Thanks to its numerous skyscrapers and lakes, Hanoi is a scenic city when viewed from above. If you're short on time, you can enjoy a sunset from a rooftop bar around Hoan Kiem Lake. It's a great way to end a busy day of exploring around the Old Quarter. But for the best views, you should head to Lotte Tower Observation Deck. This slice of modern Vietnam takes you up 65 floors for amazing 360 degree views. When we visited, there was relatively few people and plenty of space at the Lounge Bar, which offers an incredible sunset views. Hanoi is a real foodie destination with fruits, vegetables, and full meals being sold on almost every corner, both in restaurants and on the street. The best way to indulge in the local specialties is to take a street food tour, especially if you're not very familiar with the Vietnamese cuisine yet. Our guide took us to the best local spots that we never would have discovered ourselves. We tried a variety of dishes including summer rolls, egg coffee, bánh cuốn, a bánh mì sandwich, and a local favorite called bún chả. This dish of grilled pork and noodles is thought to have originated from Hanoi. We left a link to the food tour that we joined in the description below the video. We also had the chance to talk to our guide about what food you should try first when visiting. One of the most unique things you can do in Hanoi is attending a water puppet show, which is a traditional form of entertainment and cultural expression. The show takes place in a shallow pool of water where skilled puppeteers manipulate puppets with long bamboo rods hidden behind a screen. The puppets glide and dance across the water surface, which represents the rice fields where water puppetry was originally performed. The show tells various stories from Vietnamese folklore, history, and daily life. Accompanied by traditional music and singing, each scene is intricately designed with beautifully crafted puppets. We attended a show at Tong long Puppet Theater, which is considered to be the original theater in the city. They run multiple shows per day. The most interesting in place to visit was the Hỏa Lò Prison Museum, an important historic site in Hanoi. It was originally built by French colonialists in the late 19th century to jail Vietnamese revolutionaries. Later, it became a place where American prisoners of war were held during the Vietnam war. The museum provides an insight into the harsh realities of war and the resilience of the Vietnamese people. You can see the original cells and prison artifacts from the past as well as hear stories of the prisoners. A visit here paints a vivid picture of the struggles endured by those imprisoned within its walls. As we promised at the beginning of the video, we'll now share three travel tips for Hanoi. First, you need to cross the streets with confidence in the Old Quarter. In this area, the streets can be especially overwhelming with constant traffic. Instead of waiting for the right moment to cross, it's best to take the plunge and confidently walk across the street when the vehicles are moving slow. Locals told us to never hesitate, stop, or walk backwards. You have to trust that the drivers will predict and adjust their course accordingly. Looking the driver into the eyes and raising your hand also helps. Second, we recommend exploring on foot or using the grab App. Many of Hanoi's attractions, especially those in the Old Quarter, are within walking distance. Exploring the city by foot allows you to soak in the vibrant atmosphere. However, for longer distances consider using Grab, a ride hailing app popular in Southeast Asia. You can book a grab car or even opt for a motorcycle ride, which can navigate through traffic more efficiently. Finally, you should be aware of common scams. While Hanoi is generally safe, it's always wise to be cautious. Some common scams in the city include inflated prices, rigged meters for taxis, fake tour operators, or street vendors giving incorrect change. Always double check prices, use trusted tour companies, and be attentive with your money to avoid falling prey to scams. Additionally, research and be aware of any new or current scams happening in the area. This was our top selection of things to do in Hanoi. But of course there are many other activities that are worth checking out if you have more time in the city. We've included a list of those on the screen here for you. If you're planning your Vietnam trip, we recommend keeping things organized with our interactive travel planner. Find a discount code in the description below the video. We also included important links that you'll need for your Hanoi trip in the description, such as a full travel guide with where to stay and eat. We'll catch you on the next adventure.

From ancient alleyways to chic French quarters, Hanoi's neighborhoods are wildly different, and picking the wrong one could ruin your trip. In this video, I'll break down the best areas to stay in Hanoi, where to avoid, and what kind of hotels or Airbnbs are worth booking. Let's start with Hanoi's old quarter, the beating heart of the city. With over a thousand years of history, this area is packed with narrow alleys, ancient temples, street food, and buzzing energy. It's perfect for firsttime travelers, backpackers, and foodies. You'll find everything from sixth door host to $100 boutique hotels. Many right in the middle of the action. Airbnbs here are often smaller and a bit noisy. Expect character, not luxury. You're about 45 minutes from the airport, but once you're here, everything's walkable. Just a 10-minute walk south is the French Quarter. Hanoi's classy, elegant side. You will see wide boulevards, colonial architecture, and upscale shopping. Perfect for couples, luxury travelers, and anyone looking for peace without isolation. Here you'll find four to five-star hotels like Sophotel Legend Metropole, often ranging from $120 to 300 plus per night. Smaller boutique hotels are available too, starting around 70. Airbnbs here are less common, but often stylish and well-kept. Next is Deho or West Lake, an expat favorite with open space, breezes, and a slower pace. Ideal for families, digital nomads, or longer stays. Here you'll find lakefront boutique hotels and hotel chains, yoga studios, and international restaurants. Prices range from 30 to 70 for boutique stays and 100 plus for luxury lakeside hotels. Airbnbs here are popular, often modern, clean, and perfect for longer stays. It's central, only 10- 15 minutes from the city center and about 25 to 35 minutes from the airport. Bardin is Hanoi's political and cultural core. Quieter, less touristy, but close to places like the Ho Chi Min Mausoleum. It's a great area if you want fewer crowds and a local experience. Hotel options include three to fourstar business style stays, mostly priced, $30 to £80 per night. Airbnbs here can be hit or miss. Mostly local style apartments with fewer western amenities. Not great for night life, but solid for daytime exploring. Now, let's talk about where not to stay. Try to avoid far west or southern districts like Tanguan or Hardong. These areas are mostly residential and industrial with very few tourist services, long commutes, and limited English. You might save a few bucks on a hotel, but you'll waste time and energy. videos.transcript: In this video, we're going to break down our top ten things to do in Hanoi. We have been here three times in the last year, and this is our comprehensive list of some of the best things that we've experienced in one of our favorite cities in the world. This is by no means everything there is to do in Hanoi, and everybody's list is gonna look a little bit different, but we think this covers a big chunk of the best things to do in Hanoi on your first visit. So, without further ado, let's get into it. Number 10: Visit Hoan Kiem Lake. This picturesque lake in the heart of Hanoi's Old Quarter is a symbol of the city. Every day, hundreds of people flock to this lake to take photos with friends and family, walk its perimeter, catch fish, or just hang out and pass the time. The lake is famous for its eye-catching red bridge that leads from the mainland to an islet that holds the Ngoc Son Temple. Entrance to the temple is 30,000 dong per adult and 15,000 dong per child. Hoan Kiem Lake's true energy can be felt any time of the day, but the late afternoon is when it really comes alive. The photo ops are unbeatable, and it's an amazing place to take a stroll and people-watch. At night, the two main features, the red bridge and Turtle Tower, are lit up beautifully, adding to the ambiance of this already prominent feature piece in the center of Hanoi's Old Quarter. Number 9: Visit the Temple of Literature. The Temple of Literature is a stunning architectural marvel located in the center of Hanoi, surrounded by beautifully landscaped gardens. Located in Dong Da, which happens to be one of our favorite neighborhoods, this temple is one of the most well-known in the city and definitely deserves a visit. The temple is dedicated to the great philosopher Confucius and was founded in 1070 by Emperor Le Thanh Tong. This ancient complex was also the site of Vietnam's first university, which was established in 1076. The temple features five courtyards, each with unique halls, ponds, and gardens, making it the perfect backdrop for student graduation photos. The altars are popular among students who come here to pray for good grades, and notably, the temple is depicted on the 100,000 dong note. Number 8: Go On a Street Food Tour. There is no better way to get to know a culture than by getting to know its food. And there's no better way to get to know its food than by going on a street food tour. There are many food tours you can do in Hanoi, but we recommend Hanoi Street Food Tours above all of them. They have wonderful, knowledgeable guides who will take you on a culinary adventure. I went with my guide, Elena, on the adventurous food tour, but they offer an experience that caters to any diet. The tour costs about $30 per person and includes a bottle of water, one coffee, one beer, and more food than your body has room for. The tour even starts with a little happy water to get you ready to roll. If you don't know what happy water is, you're in for a treat. Come hungry, or they will have to roll you back to your hotel or hostel. Number 7: Train Street. If you're planning a trip to Hanoi, you've probably come across photos and videos of people enjoying coffee or beer while sitting on the railroad tracks as a passenger train passes by. This place is called Train Street, and it's one of the most famous and iconic tourist attractions in the city. This narrow alley is lined with cafes and shops that sell a variety of snacks, beverages, and souvenirs. The train arrives at different times each day, so it's best to check the schedule ahead of time before visiting. The locals on the street usually know when the train is coming, and they can help you, too. Many cafe owners will try to get you to come to their cafe, but as long as the train isn't coming in the next few minutes, you can walk the tracks, take photos, and not have to purchase anything. Drinks are a little bit pricier here than anywhere else, but not by much. The train only takes about three minutes to pass, and it's definitely worth experiencing. But honestly, the best part about Train Street is the amazing people watching. Number 6: Visit a Cafe! Vietnam has an incredible cafe culture, and Hanoi has hands-down some of the best cafes we have ever been to. Whether you're in the mood for a coffee or tea, need to get some work done, or just want to watch the world go by, each cafe in Vietnam has its own little vibe. Most of our time in cafes was spent working, so we didn't film too much, but we'll leave a list of our favorites down in the description. Number 5: Try Egg Coffee. While we're talking about cafes, we can't skip something that Hanoi is famous for. It sounds a bit out there, but trust us, you won't want to miss this. Egg coffee is something unique to Vietnam and, more specifically, Hanoi. It was created in the 1940s due to a milk shortage caused by the French-Indochina War. A crafty bartender named Nguyen Van Giang was looking for an alternative way to cut the bitterness of Vietnam's robusta bean by mixing egg, sugar, and a whole host of other creamy ingredients like butter, condensed milk, and even cheese. This is more of a dessert than a coffee, and it is decadent, to say the least. Our favorite place to try it is at Cafe Giang, which is the cafe created by the inventor of this creative and unique brew. Number 4: Check out a Water Puppet Show. There are a few different puppet shows in the area, but Thang Long Water Puppet Theater is a cultural gem in Hanoi. It offers a unique and traditional form of entertainment, and it's one of the oldest water puppet theaters in Vietnam, with roots dating back to the 11th century. The performances depict traditional folklore, legends, and daily life in Vietnam. And although many countries have some form of puppetry art, only Vietnam takes it to the water, and it is something you shouldn't miss if you're interested in experiencing traditional Vietnamese music and learning more about Vietnam's culture and traditions. Tickets cost anywhere from 100,000 dong to 200,000 dong for VIP seats, and you definitely want to book your tickets in advance, especially during peak tourist seasons between February and April and again from October to November. You can purchase them at the ticket office or online, although we had trouble buying them online, so we recommend walking to Hoan Kiem Lake to purchase them in person. Number 3: Hỏa Lò Prison. You can't visit Hanoi without stepping back into its dark past. Hoa Lo Prison, originally known as Maison Central and later the Hanoi Hilton was a massive prison built by the French in the late 1880s to house political prisoners during the French occupation of Vietnam. Later, during the Vietnam War, it was a place to hold Vietnamese dissidents as well as US POWs. The air inside of this unforgiving colonial prison is heavy. The museum does a really good job of transporting you back in time, and it's even better with the audio guides that can be purchased for 100,000 dong at the ticket office. We were deeply saddened to uncover the appalling mistreatment and inhumane conditions that the victims had to endure in Hanoi over the centuries. However, we strongly believe that it's important for everyone who visits this city to learn about this dark part of Vietnam's past. It may not be easy to stomach, but it provides a valuable perspective into not only the tragedy that occurred but also the admirable resilience of the Vietnamese people. Number 2: Eat Local Food! It's no secret that we LOVE Vietnamese food. It's been one of the biggest staples of our entire series in this amazing country. We've always described it as "elegant in its simplicity," and that's still how we feel to this day. Rice is a staple food ingredient in Vietnam, and the country has found numerous ways to make it more interesting. From noodles to flour, sticky to broken, Vietnamese cuisine has a remarkable ability to create a diverse range of delicious dishes using rice as its main ingredient. While most people who visit Vietnam have heard of Pho and Banh Mi, there are so many other delicacies around every corner, alley, or streetside just waiting to be discovered. One of the dishes you can't miss in Hanoi is Bun Cha. We missed it on our first visit, and rightfully so, we caught a lot of criticism in the comment section. It ended up being one of our favorite dishes, and we were kicking ourselves for not trying it the first time we were here. So, do yourself a favor and check out this staple dish of Hanoi. When visiting Hanoi, we highly recommend having lunch in Truc Bach. It's a thirty-minute walk from the Old Quarter, but it is worth the effort to eat pho cuon and pho chien phuong. These dishes go hand-in-hand and are a specialty of the small island of Truc Bach. Anyone who has seen our Hanoi videos knows how much we love this neighborhood and these specialty dishes. Almost every restaurant in this area serves these two dishes, so choose one with available seating, grab a locally brewed Truc Bach beer, and enjoy. You won't regret it. With so many great options, you're sure to find a dish that will be your new favorite. We'll leave some of our favorite restaurants and dishes linked below so you can check them out for yourself! And finally, at number one, explore the Old Quarter. Exploring Hanoi's famous Old Quarter is an essential experience for anyone visiting the city. There is something for everyone in this vibrant area, from admiring the stunning combination of French and Vietnamese architecture to visiting the many beautiful sites such as St. Joseph's Cathedral, Martyrs Monument and the various temples. Walking the streets alone could keep you busy for days, and there are plenty of other activities to fill your time here. The Old Quarter is unlike any place we've ever been in our travels, and the best time to experience it is early in the morning or late at night when local life comes alive. The real magic of Hanoi becomes apparent when you wander just off the beaten path, and a good way to avoid the bustling streets is by exploring the tranquil alleyways instead. Although it may seem chaotic at first, once you get to know the ebb-and-flow of the traffic patterns, you'll be right at home. There are a number of beautiful restaurants, cafes, street food stalls, and souvenir shops for you to enjoy and take a little piece of this incredible city home with you. Well, there you go! We really hope you liked our top ten list of things to do in Hanoi on your first visit. If you think we missed something, let us know down in the comments. If you're planning a trip to Vietnam, we just released our Vietnam Map Guide, which highlights over 100 places to visit, eat, and sleep from Hanoi all the way down to Ho Chi Minh City. It's constantly evolving as we explore more of this incredible country and you can check it out now by clicking the link in the description below. If you found this video helpful, please give it a "like," and if you're new here, don't forget to subscribe to follow along for more travel content from Southeast Asia and beyond. We'll see you in the next one! Oh, my God, it's not recording. Yes, it is. Turd.
